title: Severe Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis)
chief_complaint: My daughter has a barking cough like a seal, noisy breathing, and she's struggling to breathe. Started with a runny nose yesterday, worse tonight. She's drooling and leaning forward.

physical_exam.General: Anxious, sitting forward (tripod position), drooling, inspiratory stridor at rest, suprasternal and intercostal retractions.
physical_exam.Lungs: Barking cough. Stridor (inspiratory). Prolonged inspiration. Decreased air entry bilateral.
physical_exam.HEENT: Normal pharynx (NO trismus, NO uvular deviation, NO tonsillar asymmetry - differentiates from peritonsillar abscess).
physical_exam.Westley: Westley Croup Score 8 (severe: stridor at rest 2, retractions moderate 2, air entry decreased 2, cyanosis 2).

hidden_data.cxr: Steeple sign (subglottic narrowing) on AP neck X-ray. Normal epiglottis (differentiates from epiglottitis).
hidden_data.pulse_ox: SpO2 87% on room air, improves to 94% with O2

correct_diagnosis: Severe Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis) - Parainfluenza Virus, Westley Score 8
key_learning_points: Croup = viral infection of upper airway (larynx, trachea, bronchi). Parainfluenza virus (75%). Peak age 6 months - 3 years. Onset gradual with URI prodrome then barking cough, hoarseness, stridor

Westley Croup Score: Mild (0-2): Barking cough, no stridor at rest. Moderate (3-7): Stridor at rest, retractions. Severe (8-11): Significant stridor + retractions + decreased air entry. Impending respiratory failure (>11): Cyanosis, decreased LOC, decreased stridor (ominous sign - too weak to generate sound)

Treatment: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO/IM single dose (reduces severity, duration). All severities benefit. Nebulized Epinephrine (Racemic) 0.05mL/kg (max 0.5mL) in 3mL NS for moderate-severe stridor at rest. Observe minimum 2-4 hours post-nebulizer (rebound phenomenon)

Differentiate from epiglottitis: Croup = gradual onset, barking cough, viral prodrome. Epiglottitis = RAPID onset, toxic appearance, drooling, tripoding, NO cough (H. influenzae type B - rare due to vaccination). NEVER examine throat in suspected epiglottitis (may precipitate airway obstruction)
Hospitalize if: Stridor at rest persists >4h after steroid, ≥2 epinephrine doses needed, SpO2 <92%, dehydration, age <6 months, unreliable follow-up. PICU if impending respiratory failure
